response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend a lot of time on social media every day, and I have to tell you straight that dealing with fake content is exhausting and frankly makes me angry. At my age, after working hard all these years driving deliveries around Chicago, I do not have patience for people spreading lies and nonsense online. It worries me constantly because I see how this garbage affects everyone, including my own children. The thing that bothers me most is you cannot trust anything anymore. I have been burned before by sharing something that turned out to be completely false, and that embarrassment stays with you. Now I am very careful, maybe too careful. When I see something that seems off or too dramatic, I just scroll past it. I do not engage because honestly most people online are not worth arguing with anyway. They have already made up their minds and nothing you say will change that. What I do now is stick to a routine. I follow specific accounts that have proven themselves reliable over time, mostly local Chicago news and some Islamic community pages that I know personally verify their information. I do not click on random links from people I do not know, period. My approach might seem harsh to some, but when you have limited education like I do, you learn to be practical about things. I check if other sources are saying the same thing before I believe it. If something makes me feel too emotional right away, that is usually a red flag that someone is trying to manipulate me. The stress of constantly wondering what is real and what is fake definitely weighs on me. Some days I think about just deleting everything and going back to simpler times, but then I would lose touch with family and community news. So instead I have developed this system where I limit which groups I participate in and I never, ever share anything political or medical without being absolutely certain. It is not perfect, but it helps me sleep better at night knowing I am not part of the problem spreading misinformation to others.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 72-year-old self-employed delivery driver with primary school education, high conscientiousness, high neuroticism, and low agreeableness. These traits suggest someone who is careful and systematic but also anxious and somewhat skeptical of others. The depression and high neuroticism indicate stress and worry about the topic. Low openness suggests preference for familiar, proven methods rather than new approaches. Heavy social media use combined with limited formal education creates vulnerability to misinformation but also practical experience dealing with it. The Islamic faith and Chicago location provide specific community contexts. The tone should be direct, somewhat pessimistic, practical, and slightly irritable, reflecting low agreeableness and high neuroticism. The person would express frustration, worry, and a systematic but defensive approach to the problem.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, worried, practical, slightly irritable, and defensive with underlying anxiety
